preamble: Similar to the /s/ to /j/ shift, the transformation of /z/ to /j/ is a result of advanced yod-coalescence. The voiced alveolar fricative /z/ merges with a following /j/, typically first becoming a voiced postalveolar fricative /ʒ/ (as in 'is your...'). Further lenition can weaken this fricative to a palatal glide /j/.

examples.note: A common reduction in rapid, casual speech.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English.
